h1Title: Myeloid Sarcoma in Relapse: Symptoms, Causes & Treatment
heroOverview: Myeloid sarcoma (also called granulocytic sarcoma or chloroma) is an extramedullary tumor of immature myeloid cells that occurs in the context of acute myeloid leukemia, myelodysplastic syndromes, or myeloproliferative neoplasms. When it presents at relapse, it indicates disease that has returned following prior treatment. Population-based registries report an incidence of 1-2 cases per 100,000 inhabitants per year for AML, with myeloid sarcoma complicating 2-5% of cases. Relapsed disease often presents as a rapidly growing soft-tissue mass in skin, bone, lymph nodes, or the gastrointestinal tract, frequently accompanied by circulating blasts or marrow relapse. Treatment requires systemic reinduction chemotherapy, and outcomes remain poor with median overall survival of 6-12 months in the relapsed setting. Allogeneic stem cell transplantation offers the only potential curative pathway for eligible patients.

keyStats.prevalence: Myeloid sarcoma occurs in approximately 2-5% of AML patients; up to 25% occur at relapse rather than initial presentation
keyStats.demographics: Slight male predominance (1.2-1.5:1); occurs across all ages but peak incidence in the 6th-7th decade for AML-associated cases
keyStats.avgAge: Median age at diagnosis 55-65 years; paediatric cases less common but often associated with specific genetic subtypes (t(8;21), inv(16))
keyStats.globalCases: Estimated 0.5-1 per 100,000 annually; more commonly reported in East Asian and Middle Eastern registries due to higher AML rates

definition: Myeloid sarcoma (ICD-10: C96.9 / AML with extramedullary involvement) is defined as a tumor mass consisting of myeloid blasts occurring outside the bone marrow. It can present de novo, concurrently with AML, or as the first manifestation of relapse following prior AML treatment. The pathogenesis involves clonal myeloid blast proliferation in extramedullary sites, facilitated by adhesion molecule expression (CD56, CD44, CXCR4) that guides trafficking to tissues outside the marrow niche. Myeloid sarcoma frequently harbours the same cytogenetic abnormalities as the underlying hematologic malignancy, including t(8;21), inv(16), and complex karyotype changes. The entity is recognized in the WHO 2022 classification as a distinct entity within myeloid neoplasms. Histologically it demonstrates sheets of myeloblasts and promyelocytes that stain positive for myeloperoxidase, lysozyme, CD34, CD117, and chloroacetate esterase (Leder stain). Diagnosis requires biopsy with immunophenotyping by flow cytometry or immunohistochemistry. Management involves both local control (radiation or surgery) and systemic therapy tailored to the underlying leukemia.

typesClassification.type: Isolated myeloid sarcoma without marrow involvement (extramedullary AML)
typesClassification.description: Myeloid sarcoma as the sole disease manifestation without peripheral blood or bone marrow blasts. May represent early extramedullary spread before marrow seeding.
Myeloid sarcoma presenting simultaneously with bone marrow blasts exceeding 20%. Most common presentation pattern.
Tumor mass appearing in a patient with prior AML who achieved complete remission. Often associated with marrow relapse and carries a poor prognosis.

typesClassification.type: Myeloid sarcoma in myelodysplastic syndrome or myeloproliferative neoplasm
typesClassification.description: Rare presentation in MDS or MPN without concurrent AML; indicates disease progression and typically rapid transformation to AML.
Myeloid sarcoma as the presenting feature with no prior hematologic diagnosis; may represent undiagnosed AML or evolve rapidly to systemic disease.

primarySymptoms: Rapidly enlarging, firm, non-tender soft-tissue mass in skin, subcutaneous tissue, or muscle, often described as a bruise-like or violaceous nodule.
Painful swelling of the jaw, orbit, or paranasal sinuses from myeloid sarcoma involving craniofacial bones in approximately 30-40% of cases.
Spinal cord compression or radicular pain from vertebral body or epidural myeloid sarcoma, presenting with back pain, lower limb weakness, or urinary retention.
Gastrointestinal obstruction, bleeding, or palpable mass from intestinal myeloid sarcoma affecting the stomach, small bowel, or colon.
Lymph node enlargement with or without systemic B-symptoms (fever, night sweats, weight loss) in nodal disease.
Testicular pain and swelling in male patients with myeloid sarcoma involving the gonads.
Rapidly progressive neurological deficits including cranial nerve palsies when myeloid sarcoma involves the central nervous system.
Cutaneous papules, nodules, or plaques that may resemble other dermatologic conditions such as leukemia cutis or lymphoma.
Peripheral blood showing circulating blasts, anemia, thrombocytopenia, or leukocytosis in the context of prior AML.
Bone pain and pathological fracture when sarcoma involves long bones or ribs.
earlyWarningSigns: New rapidly growing soft-tissue mass in a patient with a history of AML or MDS
Recurrence of B-symptoms (fever, night sweats, weight loss) following remission
Spinal pain with neurological signs suggesting epidural mass
Cutaneous nodules with atypical myeloid cells on skin biopsy
Rising peripheral blood blast count on routine monitoring
Unexplained cytopenias in a patient with prior hematologic malignancy
emergencySigns: Spinal cord compression from epidural myeloid sarcoma — requires urgent MRI and high-dose dexamethasone with emergency radiation or surgical decompression
Airway obstruction from head and neck myeloid sarcoma involving the orbit, sinuses, or parapharyngeal space
Gastrointestinal perforation or massive hemorrhage from bowel involvement
Acute neurological deterioration from intracranial myeloid sarcoma with mass effect
Rapid blast proliferation with leukostasis syndrome requiring urgent leukapheresis and chemotherapy

causes.cause: Relapse of previously treated acute myeloid leukemia
causes.description: Myeloid sarcoma at relapse results from persistence of AML blasts in extramedullary sanctuary sites that survived prior chemotherapy. Relapse typically occurs within 2 years of initial remission. Minimal residual disease (MRD) testing identifies high-risk patients before overt relapse.

AML blasts expressing specific adhesion molecules (CD56 neural cell adhesion molecule, CXCR4 chemokine receptor, CD44 hyaluronic acid receptor) have enhanced capacity to migrate and engraft in extramedullary tissues. This trafficking pattern is influenced by the bone marrow niche and chemokine gradients.

Specific AML cytogenetics are associated with increased myeloid sarcoma risk, including t(8;21)(q22;q22) RUNX1::RUNX1T1, inv(16)(p13q22) CBFB::MYH11, t(6;11)(q27;q23) MLL::AF6, and complex karyotype. These genetic lesions alter expression of trafficking molecules that guide blasts to non-marrow sites.

Insufficient anthracycline dose, suboptimal consolidation, or failure to consolidate with allogeneic stem cell transplant in high-risk AML predisposes to both marrow and extramedullary relapse. MRD-positivity by multiparameter flow cytometry or NGS predicts increased relapse risk.

Patients who relapse after allogeneic stem cell transplantation have a high rate of extramedullary involvement due to graft-versus-leukemia effect failure. Donor-derived or recipient-derived blasts can establish sanctuary-site tumors resistant to donor lymphocyte infusion.

riskFactors.factor: Prior AML diagnosis (especially with adverse cytogenetics)
riskFactors.description: History of AML, particularly with complex karyotype, FLT3-ITD, or TP53 mutation, carries the highest risk. Relapse occurs in approximately 50-60% of patients achieving first remission.
Older patients have inferior remission rates and higher cumulative incidence of extramedullary relapse after chemotherapy alone.
CD56 (neural cell adhesion molecule) expression on AML blasts is significantly associated with extramedullary involvement and myeloid sarcoma formation.
Hyperleukocytosis at diagnosis correlates with higher extramedullary disease risk, possibly through increased blast trafficking to distant tissues.
Patients who receive only 1-2 cycles of consolidation chemotherapy without allogeneic transplant in adverse-risk disease have higher relapse rates.

riskFactors.factor: Graft failure or early relapse after transplant
riskFactors.description: Early relapse after allogeneic transplant is strongly associated with extramedullary sanctuary disease including myeloid sarcoma.

affectedDemographics: AML patients of any age who have achieved prior remission — myeloid sarcoma accounts for up to 25% of all relapses in some series.
Males have a slight predominance (1.2-1.5:1 ratio) across most age groups.
Median age at relapse is 55-65 years, reflecting the age distribution of AML overall.
Children with core-binding factor AML (t(8;21) or inv(16)) are at elevated risk and may present with myeloid sarcoma as an initial manifestation.
Patients in East Asia and the Middle East show higher reported incidence, reflecting regional differences in AML prevalence.
diagnosisOverview: Diagnosis requires a high index of suspicion in any patient with prior AML presenting with a new mass or unexplained symptoms. MRI of the symptomatic site is the preferred imaging for soft-tissue or CNS involvement, while CT detects thoracic, abdominal, or pelvic masses. Fluorodeoxyglucose PET-CT (FDG-PET) is highly sensitive for detecting occult extramedullary disease and is recommended for staging in relapsed AML. The diagnostic gold standard is core needle or incisional biopsy with histology (H&E staining showing myeloblasts with high nuclear-to-cytoplasmic ratio and fine chromatin), special stains (myeloperoxidase, chloroacetate esterase/Leder stain, and periodic acid-Schiff), and immunophenotyping by immunohistochemistry (CD34, CD117, MPO, lysozyme, CD68, CD163) or flow cytometry. Cytogenetic analysis of the biopsy tissue and concurrent bone marrow aspirate/biopsy are mandatory to guide treatment. When marrow is not involved, diagnosis of isolated extramedullary relapse requires systemic work-up with PET-CT and lumbar puncture for CNS staging. Differential diagnosis includes non-Hodgkin lymphoma, Hodgkin lymphoma, metastatic carcinoma, sarcoma, and inflammatory pseudotumor. Myeloid sarcoma frequently coexists with active marrow disease or subsequent marrow relapse, requiring systemic therapy regardless of local disease burden.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Defines extent of soft-tissue, CNS, or spinal myeloid sarcoma; essential for surgical or radiation planning
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: 30-60 minute scan; gadolinium contrast often used; results available within 24-48 hours.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Detects occult extramedullary disease at multiple sites; more sensitive than CT alone for systemic staging
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Whole-body scan, 90 minutes including contrast injection and uptake period; results in 24-48 hours.

diagnosticTests.test: Core biopsy with histology and immunophenotyping
diagnosticTests.purpose: Confirms myeloid lineage of the extramedullary mass; differentiates from lymphoma, carcinoma, and sarcoma
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Local anesthesia; 18-gauge core biopsy, 2-3 cores; results in 3-5 days with special stains.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Characterizes immunophenotype of blasts (CD34, CD117, MPO, CD13, CD33, CD56) and establishes clonal relationship to prior AML
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Fresh tissue submitted in transport medium; 24-48 hours for results.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Determines whether marrow relapse is present alongside extramedullary disease; essential for treatment planning
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Iliac crest biopsy under local anesthesia; results in 24-48 hours; must be done before reinduction chemotherapy.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies recurrent abnormalities (t(8;21), inv(16), FLT3-ITD, NPM1, TP53) that guide therapy; MRD monitoring
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Bone marrow sample for conventional karyotype, FISH, and NGS panel; results in 1-2 weeks.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Excludes CNS involvement in patients with intracranial or head and neck myeloid sarcoma
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Bedside procedure; CSF for cytology and flow cytometry; results within 24-48 hours.

diagnosticTests.test: Full blood count and peripheral blood smear
diagnosticTests.purpose: Detects circulating blasts, cytopenias, or leukocytosis that may accompany relapse
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Standard blood test; results within hours.

treatmentOverview: Management of relapsed myeloid sarcoma requires coordinated local and systemic therapy. Systemic reinduction is the cornerstone and should begin promptly once the diagnosis is confirmed. For patients with relapsed AML who are chemo-sensitive (duration of first remission >12 months, no prior transplant), reinduction with FLAG-Ida (fludarabine, cytarabine, idarubicin, filgrastim), MEC (mitoxantrone, etoposide, cytarabine), or clofarabine-based regimens achieves second remission in 40-60%. For those with early relapse or transplant-experienced disease, hypomethylating agents (azacitidine, decitabine) with venetoclax, or targeted agents (FLT3 inhibitors gilteritinib, midostaurin; IDH inhibitors enasidenib, ivosidenib) are increasingly used and have improved outcomes in molecularly selected subgroups. Allogeneic stem cell transplantation in second remission offers the only curative pathway; conditioning regimens may be myeloablative or reduced intensity depending on patient age and comorbidities. Local therapy includes surgical resection for accessible symptomatic masses and definitive radiation (24-30 Gy in 12 fractions) for spinal cord compression, CNS involvement, or residual disease after chemotherapy. Donor lymphocyte infusion may be used for post-transplant relapse. Novel approaches including bispecific antibodies (blinatumomab for B-lineage, gemtuzumab ozogamicin for CD33+ disease), ADC therapy (gemtuzumab ozogamicin), and checkpoint inhibitors are under active investigation. Palliative care should be integrated early for patients not proceeding to transplant.

medicalTreatments.name: FLAG-Ida reinduction chemotherapy (fludarabine, cytarabine, idarubicin, filgrastim)
medicalTreatments.description: Intensive salvage regimen for relapsed AML with prior remission duration >6 months; achieves CR2 in 50-60% in transplant-eligible patients.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: CR2 rate 45-65%; median OS 8-14 months in relapsed setting; best outcomes when followed by allogeneic transplant

medicalTreatments.name: Azacitidine or decitabine with venetoclax
medicalTreatments.description: Hypomethylating agent plus BCL-2 inhibitor; increasingly used in older or comorbidity-burdened patients with relapsed AML including myeloid sarcoma.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Overall response rate 30-50%; better tolerated than intensive chemotherapy; CR/CRi rates 20-35%

medicalTreatments.name: Gilteritinib (FLT3 inhibitor)
medicalTreatments.description: Approved for FLT3-mutated relapsed/refractory AML; crosses blood-brain barrier and has activity in CNS myeloid sarcoma with FLT3 mutation.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Median OS 9-12 months in FLT3-mutated relapsed AML; superior to salvage chemotherapy in the ADMIRAL trial

medicalTreatments.name: Gemtuzumab ozogamicin (anti-CD33 ADC)
medicalTreatments.description: CD33-directed antibody-drug conjugate; active in CD33+ myeloid sarcoma as monotherapy or in combination with non-intensive regimens.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Overall response rate 30% in monotherapy; synergistic with hypomethylating agents

medicalTreatments.name: Allogeneic stem cell transplantation in CR2
medicalTreatments.description: Only curative option for relapsed myeloid sarcoma; preferred haploidentical or matched unrelated donor transplant in second remission.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: 3-year OS 30-45% for patients in CR2 receiving transplant; lower for active disease at transplant

medicalTreatments.name: Venetoclax combinations with azacitidine or low-dose cytarabine
medicalTreatments.description: Non-intensive outpatient regimen suitable for patients ineligible for transplant; synergistic pro-apoptotic effect.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: ORR 40-50% in relapsed AML; increasingly used as bridge to transplant in responders

medicalTreatments.name: Inotuzumab ozogamicin or blinatumomab (investigational)
medicalTreatments.description: For CD22+ or lymphoid-antigen expressing myeloid sarcoma variants; bispecific antibody constructs have activity in mixed-phenotype acute leukemia.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Case series and phase I/II data show 20-40% CR rates; enrollment in clinical trials encouraged

medicalTreatments.name: Radiation therapy 24-30 Gy in 12 fractions
medicalTreatments.description: Definitive or palliative local control for symptomatic myeloid sarcoma masses, especially spinal cord compression, CNS disease, or residual post-chemotherapy disease.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Local control rate 70-85%; rapid symptom relief in cord compression; often used as bridge to systemic therapy

surgicalOptions.name: Surgical resection of accessible symptomatic masses
surgicalOptions.description: Debulking or complete resection of symptomatic soft-tissue or visceral myeloid sarcoma that causes obstruction, pain, or functional compromise and is surgically accessible.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Symptom relief in 60-80% of cases; often followed by radiation to the surgical bed to reduce local recurrence

surgicalOptions.name: Emergency spinal decompression for cord compression
surgicalOptions.description: Laminectomy and decompression for epidural myeloid sarcoma causing rapid-onset myelopathy; usually followed by radiation and systemic therapy.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Neurological improvement in 50-70% if performed within 24-48 hours of deficit onset; better outcomes in incomplete paraparesis than complete paralysis

surgicalOptions.name: Orbital or craniofacial debulking
surgicalOptions.description: Resection of craniofacial myeloid sarcoma causing proptosis, cranial nerve palsy, or airway obstruction; often combined with post-operative radiation.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Preservation of vision and orbital function in 60-75%; complete resection rarely achievable due to infiltrative nature

alternativeTreatments.name: Donor lymphocyte infusion (DLI)
alternativeTreatments.description: For post-transplant relapse; elicits graft-versus-leukemia effect; can be combined with hypomethylating agents or targeted therapy to enhance response.

alternativeTreatments.name: Clinical trial enrollment (novel agents, CAR-T, bispecific antibodies)
alternativeTreatments.description: Relapsed myeloid sarcoma has limited approved options beyond salvage chemotherapy and transplantation; clinical trials of novel agents offer access to promising therapies.

alternativeTreatments.name: Palliative radiation for symptomatic control
alternativeTreatments.description: Low-dose radiation (8-20 Gy in 4-10 fractions) for pain or functional impairment from bulky symptomatic disease in patients not candidates for systemic therapy.

whySeeSpecialist: Relapsed myeloid sarcoma requires urgent evaluation by a hematologist-oncologist with AML and transplant expertise to coordinate systemic reinduction, donor identification, and local therapy. Treatment decisions are complex and require assessment of cytogenetics, MRD status, prior therapy, and patient fitness. Multidisciplinary input from transplant physicians, radiation oncologists, neurosurgeons, and palliative care is essential.
doctorSelectionGuide: Seek a tertiary leukemia or transplant centre with high-volume AML experience, on-site transplant programme, FLT3/IDH inhibitor availability, and CAR-T or clinical trial programmes. Ask about their experience with myeloid sarcoma specifically, their approach to relapsed AML, and transplant outcomes for second-remission patients.
hospitalCriteria: Accredited stem cell transplant programme with haploidentical and matched unrelated donor capability
On-site radiation oncology with CNS and spine radiation expertise
Molecular diagnostic laboratory with FLT3, IDH, NPM1, TP53 profiling
AML clinical trial programme and phase I unit access
Neuro-oncology and neurosurgery for CNS involvement
keyFacilities: Tertiary leukemia centres with transplant programmes
Haematopathology with flow cytometry and cytogenetics
Molecular diagnostics with NGS for MRD monitoring
Palliative care integrated into haematology-oncology service
preventionStrategies: Achieve deep remission (MRD-negative) with intensive consolidation therapy in first CR, including allogeneic transplant for adverse-risk AML.
Use FLT3 inhibitors (midostaurin, gilteritinib) and IDH inhibitors (enasidenib, ivosidenib) as maintenance or post-transplant consolidation in molecularly selected patients.
Monitor MRD by multiparameter flow cytometry or NGS every 3 months for 2 years after remission; intervene with pre-emptive therapy when MRD turns positive.
Maintain prophylaxis against CNS relapse with intrathecal chemotherapy during salvage regimens if prior CNS involvement.
Use donor lymphocyte infusion early after post-transplant relapse before florid disease progression.
lifestyleModifications: Maintain strict infection precautions during neutropenic periods following reinduction chemotherapy.
Avoid crowded indoor spaces and raw/uncooked foods during the 4-6 weeks of neutropenia after intensive chemotherapy.
Engage in gentle physical activity during recovery but avoid contact sports when platelet counts are low.
Prioritize adequate nutrition with protein intake of 1.2-1.5 g/kg/day to support immune recovery and maintain muscle mass.
Seek psychological support and counselling for the significant emotional burden of relapsed AML; consider joining patient support groups.

recommended: High-protein diet (1.2-1.5 g/kg/day) to support hematopoietic recovery and prevent muscle wasting
Neutropenic diet: cooked foods only, no raw fish or unpasteurized products during neutropenia
Small frequent meals to maintain caloric intake during chemotherapy-related nausea
Iron-rich foods if anaemic (red meat, beans, fortified cereals) and after transplant
avoid: Raw or undercooked meat, fish, eggs, and unpasteurized dairy products during neutropenia
Grapefruit and Seville oranges (significant CYP3A4 interaction with many AML drugs)
Excessive alcohol during intensive chemotherapy and transplant (hepatotoxicity risk)
High-sodium processed foods to manage fluid retention from supportive medications

exerciseGuidelines: Light activity (walking, stretching) is encouraged during recovery from chemotherapy. Avoid strenuous exercise, contact sports, and swimming when neutrophils or platelets are low. Gradually increase intensity as blood counts recover. Pulmonary rehabilitation is not typically required unless pre-existing lung disease is present.
dailyManagement: Take all prescribed supportive medications: antiemetics, infection prophylaxis (cotrimoxazole, acyclovir, fluconazole), and growth factor support (filgrastim) as directed.
Monitor temperature at home and report fever above 38.0°C promptly for neutropenic fever assessment.
Check blood counts as instructed; avoid exposure to anyone with active infection.
Maintain meticulous oral hygiene with soft-bristled toothbrush and alcohol-free mouthwash.
Report new soft-tissue masses, bone pain, neurological symptoms, or any constitutional symptoms to the haematology team immediately.
prognosis: Relapsed myeloid sarcoma carries a poor prognosis with median overall survival of 6-12 months without allogeneic stem cell transplantation. Outcomes are better in patients who achieve CR2 and proceed to transplant (median OS 12-18 months; 3-year OS 25-40%). Poorer prognostic factors include: relapse within 12 months of first CR, active marrow disease at diagnosis of myeloid sarcoma, FLT3-ITD or TP53 mutation, prior transplant, age over 60, and poor response to salvage therapy. Patients with isolated extramedullary relapse (no marrow involvement) have a slightly better prognosis if MRD remains negative in the marrow. Novel targeted agents and immunotherapies are beginning to improve outcomes in molecularly selected subgroups.
recoveryTimeline: Neutropenia following intensive salvage chemotherapy typically lasts 3-4 weeks; neutrophil recovery is supported by filgrastim. Platelet recovery follows over 4-6 weeks. Complete remission (CR2) by morphology is assessed by bone marrow biopsy at day 28-35 post-chemotherapy. Full hematologic recovery may take 6-8 weeks. Patients proceeding to transplant require an additional 3-6 months for full engraftment and early transplant complications to resolve.
complications: Bone marrow failure with neutropenic sepsis, opportunistic infection (fungal, viral), and bleeding — managed with growth factors, antimicrobials, and transfusion support.
Spinal cord compression with permanent paraplegia or quadriplegia if not treated urgently — requires MRI, steroids, and emergency decompression.
CNS involvement with seizures, altered consciousness, or stroke-like deficits — requires intrathecal chemotherapy and/or whole-brain radiation.
Gastrointestinal obstruction or perforation from intestinal myeloid sarcoma — requires emergency surgical intervention.
Graft-versus-host disease in patients post-allogeneic transplant — ranges from skin and liver involvement to life-threatening gastrointestinal disease.
Tumor lysis syndrome during rapid blast kill — requires vigorous hydration, allopurinol or rasburicase, and electrolyte monitoring.
Chemotherapy-induced cardiotoxicity (anthracyclines) and hepatotoxicity — monitor with echocardiography and liver function tests.
Progressive disease with blast proliferation despite salvage therapy — consider hospice referral and palliative symptom management.

supportResources.name: Leukaemia & Lymphoma Research / Blood Cancer UK — myeloid sarcoma information
supportResources.description: UK patient charity providing information on myeloid sarcoma, AML relapse, and transplant with support helpline.

supportResources.name: American Cancer Society — Acute Myeloid Leukemia
supportResources.description: Comprehensive patient information on AML including myeloid sarcoma, treatment options, and clinical trial matching.

supportResources.name: National Marrow Donor Program / Be The Match — transplant information
supportResources.description: US resource for stem cell transplant information, donor matching, and patient support for AML relapse.

supportResources.name: Leukemia Patient Advocates Foundation — global AML resources
supportResources.description: International patient advocacy network providing resources, clinical trial information, and peer support for AML patients.

confusedWithConditions.name: Acute Myeloid Leukemia
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: AML presents with marrow involvement, peripheral blood blasts, and cytopenias. Myeloid sarcoma is extramedullary disease that may occur with or without concurrent marrow involvement. The key distinction is anatomic location: marrow versus soft-tissue or visceral sites.

confusedWithConditions.name: Non-Hodgkin Lymphoma
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: NHL presents with lymphadenopathy and B-symptoms; histology shows lymphoid markers (CD20, PAX5) rather than myeloid markers (MPO, CD34). Immunophenotyping by flow cytometry or IHC establishes the correct diagnosis.

confusedWithConditions.name: Metastatic Soft Tissue Sarcoma
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Sarcoma presents as a primary soft-tissue mass that may metastasize; myeloid sarcoma is a manifestation of systemic hematologic malignancy with characteristic myeloid immunophenotype. Medical history and systemic staging distinguish the two.

relatedConditions.name: Acute Myeloid Leukemia
relatedConditions.relevance: Myeloid sarcoma is almost always a manifestation of AML or its relapse. All treatment decisions are guided by the underlying leukemia biology, cytogenetics, and MRD status.

relatedConditions.name: Myelodysplastic Syndrome
relatedConditions.relevance: MDS can transform to AML and present with myeloid sarcoma; this usually signals imminent leukemic transformation and requires prompt systemic therapy.

relatedConditions.name: Myeloproliferative Neoplasms
relatedConditions.relevance: MPN in accelerated phase, particularly CML in blast crisis, can present with myeloid sarcoma. Distinction from de novo AML is essential for targeted therapy.

faqs.question: What is myeloid sarcoma?
faqs.answer: Myeloid sarcoma is an extramedullary tumor of immature myeloid cells (myeloblasts) that develops outside the bone marrow. It can occur with AML, at relapse, or rarely as an isolated tumor without marrow involvement.

faqs.question: What is the difference between myeloid sarcoma and AML?
faqs.answer: AML primarily involves the bone marrow and peripheral blood with circulating blasts. Myeloid sarcoma is the same disease manifesting as a solid tumor in soft tissues, organs, or lymph nodes outside the marrow. The two often coexist.

faqs.question: What causes myeloid sarcoma?
faqs.answer: Myeloid sarcoma results from clonal AML blasts expressing adhesion molecules (CD56, CXCR4) that enable migration and engraftment in extramedullary tissues. Prior AML treatment failure allows blast persistence in sanctuary sites.

faqs.question: What are the symptoms of myeloid sarcoma?
faqs.answer: Symptoms include a rapidly growing firm mass in skin, muscle, or soft tissue; jaw or orbital swelling; spinal cord compression with back pain and leg weakness; bowel obstruction; and lymph node enlargement with B-symptoms.

faqs.question: How is myeloid sarcoma diagnosed?
faqs.answer: Diagnosis requires biopsy of the mass with histology (H&E), special stains (MPO, chloroacetate esterase), and immunophenotyping by IHC or flow cytometry to confirm myeloid lineage. Bone marrow biopsy and systemic staging with PET-CT are mandatory.

faqs.question: Is myeloid sarcoma cancer?
faqs.answer: Yes. Myeloid sarcoma is cancer — it is a manifestation of acute myeloid leukemia (a blood and bone marrow cancer). It is also called granulocytic sarcoma or chloroma.

faqs.question: What is the prognosis for relapsed myeloid sarcoma?
faqs.answer: Relapsed myeloid sarcoma has a poor prognosis with median overall survival of 6-12 months without transplant. Outcomes improve significantly for patients who achieve a second remission and proceed to allogeneic stem cell transplantation.

faqs.question: How is relapsed myeloid sarcoma treated?
faqs.answer: Treatment combines systemic reinduction chemotherapy (FLAG-Ida, MEC, or azacitidine-venetoclax), local radiation or surgery for symptomatic masses, and consolidation with allogeneic stem cell transplant in eligible patients.

faqs.question: Can myeloid sarcoma be cured?
faqs.answer: Cure is possible only through allogeneic stem cell transplantation in second remission. Transplant achieves long-term survival in 25-40% of patients; non-transplant approaches are palliative.

faqs.question: Is myeloid sarcoma painful?
faqs.answer: Most myeloid sarcoma masses are painless initially but become tender as they enlarge. Spinal or epidural disease causes severe back pain and neurological symptoms. Rapid growth can cause pain from mass effect on surrounding structures.

faqs.question: Does myeloid sarcoma show up on PET scan?
faqs.answer: Yes. Myeloid sarcoma is typically FDG-avid and lights up on PET-CT. PET-CT is the most sensitive imaging modality for detecting occult extramedullary disease and is recommended for systemic staging in relapsed AML.

faqs.question: Can myeloid sarcoma occur without AML?
faqs.answer: Rarely. Isolated myeloid sarcoma without concurrent marrow disease accounts for fewer than 5% of cases. Most patients either have known AML or develop systemic disease within weeks to months of the extramedullary presentation.

faqs.question: What is chloroma?
faqs.answer: Chloroma (green tumor) is an older name for myeloid sarcoma, derived from the Greek 'chloros' (green). The green coloration is caused by myeloperoxidase in the leukemic cells. It is used interchangeably with myeloid sarcoma in the medical literature.

faqs.question: Is myeloid sarcoma treatable?
faqs.answer: Yes. Systemic chemotherapy, targeted agents (FLT3 inhibitors, IDH inhibitors), radiation, surgery, and allogeneic transplant are all effective treatment modalities. The key is rapid diagnosis and initiation of therapy before blast proliferation causes organ damage.

faqs.question: How common is myeloid sarcoma in AML?
faqs.answer: Myeloid sarcoma occurs in approximately 2-5% of AML patients. At relapse, the proportion increases — up to 25% of relapses present with or include extramedullary disease.

faqs.question: Can myeloid sarcoma spread to the brain?
faqs.answer: Yes. CNS involvement is a recognized complication of myeloid sarcoma, manifesting with cranial nerve palsies, headaches, seizures, or stroke-like symptoms. CNS involvement requires intrathecal chemotherapy and often whole-brain radiation.

faqs.question: What is the life expectancy for relapsed myeloid sarcoma?
faqs.answer: Without transplant, median survival is 6-12 months. With allogeneic transplant in CR2, median survival extends to 12-18 months and 3-year survival reaches 25-40%. Molecularly targeted agents are improving these numbers.

faqs.question: Does myeloid sarcoma always mean the AML has returned?
faqs.answer: In the setting of prior AML, myeloid sarcoma almost always indicates relapse. Isolated myeloid sarcoma without prior AML history may represent an early form of AML or a prelude to systemic disease and requires urgent work-up.

faqs.question: Can radiation treat myeloid sarcoma?
faqs.answer: Radiation provides effective local control for symptomatic myeloid sarcoma masses. It is used for spinal cord compression, CNS involvement, residual post-chemotherapy disease, and palliative symptom control. It is always combined with systemic therapy.

faqs.question: Should family members be tested if myeloid sarcoma is diagnosed?
faqs.answer: Routine family screening is not recommended. However, first-degree relatives should be aware of the diagnosis and report any concerning symptoms. Genetic counselling is appropriate if there is a suspected inherited leukaemia predisposition syndrome.

faqs.question: How quickly does myeloid sarcoma grow?
faqs.answer: Myeloid sarcoma is often rapidly progressive; masses can double in size over 1-2 weeks. Rapid growth and organ dysfunction can develop within weeks without treatment, making prompt diagnosis and therapy initiation critical.

symptomKeywords: rapidly growing soft tissue mass AML
metaTitle: Myeloid Sarcoma in Relapse: Symptoms, Treatment & Prognosis | aihealz
metaDescription: Relapsed myeloid sarcoma: extramedullary AML, treatment options, transplant, prognosis. Medically reviewed guide.
